coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and copper (Ⅱ) co-doped carbon dots as multi-functional fluorescent probes for Fe3+ ions and tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jing
Chen, Qiumeng
Zhang, Zhixiao
Wang, Zhaoli
Gong, Zhengjun
coredata.dc:description: This paper synthesized carbon dots (Cu-NCDs) doped with nitrogen and copper, which could be used as a sensor for measurement of Fe3+ and tetracycline (TC). The average particle sizes of CDs and Cu-NCDs were determined as 3.9 nm and 4.8 nm, respectively. The presence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and Cu atoms was confirmed in the structure of Cu-NCDs. Both CDs and Cu-NCDs exhibited a strong fluorescence (FL) emission peak at 450 nm, but the doping of Cu and N makes the surface of Cu-NCDs positively charged, which enhances the fluorescence intensity. The carbon dots doped with copper had more significant optical effects than those doped with other metals, and the relative quantum yield (QY) of the Cu-NCDs was as high as 53.6%. Cu-NCDs can be used as multi-functional fluorescent probes to selectively detect Fe3+ and TC based on fluorescence quenching. The limit of detection of Fe3+ and TC separately was found as 1.189 µM and 0.416 µM. This probe is also suitable for the indirect determination of Fe2+ because the Fe2+ is oxidated to Fe3+ by H2O2. The mechanisms of the quenching process for Fe3+ and TC were explored that is the complex process of dynamic quenching and static quenching based on internal filtration effect (IFE), respectively. The fabricated sensors were successfully applied to determine Fe3+ and TC in tap and lake water samples, suggesting a promising application in environment analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen and copper (II) co-doping
Fluorometric sensor
Fe3+
TC
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: FL detection of Fe3+ and TC
Detection of Fe3+ and TC in actual samples
Fe3+ and TC sensing with Cu-NCDs

Thanks to the Analytical and Testing Center of Southwest Jiaotong University for the support of experimental conditions.

This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22176154 & 21777131) and Science and Technology Department Foundation of Sichuan Province (No. 22ZDYF1945). Thanks to the Analytical and Testing Center of Southwest Jiaotong University for the support of experimental conditions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation process for Cu-NCDs.
TEM image of the Cu-NCDs (a) and CDs (b), the insets are the corresponding size distribution histogram.
(a) XPS spectra and the high-resolution C1s (b), N1s (c), O1s (e), Cu2p (f) spectrum of Cu-NCDs. (d) FT-IR spectra of Cu-NCDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence excitation spectrum spectra (fluorescence emission at 450 nm). The 3D fluorescence spectrogram of the Cu-NCDs (b), the 3D fluorescence spectrogram of the Cu-NCDs with Fe3+ (c), and TC (d).

Effect of solution pH with Fe3+ (a) and TC (d); Effect of reaction time with Fe3+ (b) and TC (f); Effect of temperature with Fe3+ (c) and TC (h). The concentration of Fe3+ and TC was 300 μM and 16.7 μM, respectively. (F 0 : Fluorescence intensity of the Cu-NCDs, F 1: The fluorescence intensity after adding the detection target to the Cu-NCDs).

FL spectra of the Cu-NCDs for different Fe3+ concentrations over the range 10–1500 μM (a) and TC from 1 μM to 80 μM (c). A linear relationship between F 0/F 1 and Fe3+ concentration over the range 10–1500 μM (b) and between F 0/F 1 and TC (1 to 80 μM (d)). The anti-interference ability of the Cu-NCDs to different ions in the presence of Fe3+ (e) and TC (f). Conditions: For Fe3+: pH = 4, 30 min of reaction time, 20 ℃ of reaction temperature; For TC: pH = 10, 30 min of reaction time, 20 ℃ of reaction temperature for TC.
FL spectra of the Cu-NCDs and UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe3+ (a) and TC (b). FL decay curves of the Cu-NCDs before and after adding Fe3+ (c) and TC (d).
Stern-Volmer quenching curves of Cu-NCDs with Fe3+ (a) and TC (b) under the different temperatures.
Quenching constants of Cu-NCDs with Fe3 + and TC under the different temperatures.
Comparison of different fluorescent methods for Fe3+ and TC sensing.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and copper (Ⅱ) co-doped carbon dots as multi-functional fluorescent probes for Fe3+ ions and tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu 611756, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu 611756, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chengdu Academy of Environmental Sciences, Chengdu 610072, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chengdu Academy of Environmental Sciences, Chengdu 610072, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State-province Joint Engineering Laboratory of Spatial Information Technology of High-Speed Rail Safety, Chengdu 611756, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State-province Joint Engineering Laboratory of Spatial Information Technology of High-Speed Rail Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State-province Joint Engineering Laboratory of Spatial Information Technology of High-Speed Rail Safety, Chengdu 611756, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper synthesized carbon dots (Cu-NCDs) doped with nitrogen and copper, which could be used as a sensor for measurement of Fe3+ and tetracycline (TC). The average particle sizes of CDs and Cu-NCDs were determined as 3.9 nm and 4.8 nm, respectively. The presence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and Cu atoms was confirmed in the structure of Cu-NCDs. Both CDs and Cu-NCDs exhibited a strong fluorescence (FL) emission peak at 450 nm, but the doping of Cu and N makes the surface of Cu-NCDs positively charged, which enhances the fluorescence intensity. The carbon dots doped with copper had more significant optical effects than those doped with other metals, and the relative quantum yield (QY) of the Cu-NCDs was as high as 53.6%. Cu-NCDs can be used as multi-functional fluorescent probes to selectively detect Fe3+ and TC based on fluorescence quenching. The limit of detection of Fe3+ and TC separately was found as 1.189 µM and 0.416 µM. This probe is also suitable for the indirect determination of Fe2+ because the Fe2+ is oxidated to Fe3+ by H2O2. The mechanisms of the quenching process for Fe3+ and TC were explored that is the complex process of dynamic quenching and static quenching based on internal filtration effect (IFE), respectively. The fabricated sensors were successfully applied to determine Fe3+ and TC in tap and lake water samples, suggesting a promising application in environment analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely explored in recent years because of their excellent optical properties, superior performance, ultra-small size, outstanding biocompatibility, low toxicity and et al [1–4]. Compared with pure CDs, functionally modified carbon dots have attracted much attention due to their novel and unique optical properties and used in analytical detection [5], biological imaging [6], light-emitting devices [7], photocatalysis technology [8] and drug delivery [9]. Heteroatomic doping and surface functionalization are effective methods for modulating the optical properties of CDs. They can be relatively convenient to change the electronic structures related to band gap of CDs. CDs have different inherent properties, and therefore better performance in analytical applications, such as solvent, ion, pH and organic small molecule determination, et al. [10–12].

To date, most of CDs doped with non-metallic heteroatoms, including N [13], S [14], B [15] and P [16], which have been applied in metal ion detection, temperature sensing, and cell imaging.However, metallic heteroatoms have more electrons and vacant orbitals than non-metallic heteroatoms, so the energy gap related electronic structures of metal-doped CDs are easier to change, providing more active sites and bringing the doped CDs with novel functions [17,18]. At present, researches on metal ions doped CDs such as Cu [19], Zn [20], Mg [21], Gd [22], Ce [23] and Co [24], have been reported. For example, Yuan et al. [25] prepared Fe-CDs to detect dopamine in Hala cells, which may be attributed to the formation of a multiplex complex Fe3+ tissue due to coordination, oxidation and production of hydroquinone and quenching the fluorescence of CDs. Unfortunately, certain metals are harmful to organisms, so there is an urgent need to develop less toxic metal-doped CDs.

Due to the good safety and easy interaction with the surface functional groups of CDs, the copper have also attracted much attention, and was used to be doped with CDs [26]. For example, the surface traps on the prepared Cu-CDs were effectively passivated by functional groups, like C-N and N-Cu–N, and only the radiative transition of sp2 carbon produced fluorescence emission. A single recombination mode leaded to the excitation-independent fluorescence behavior. Therefore, the Cu-CDs was served as a fluorescent probe with the excitation-independent fluorescence property [27]. In addition to metal doping, non-metal doping has also received widespread attention. N as a typical non-metal doping atom, it can make CDs have a positively charged surface and significantly improve the interaction between CDs and analytes [28,29]. Therefore, we choose nitrogen and copper as doping atoms to synthesize Cu-NCDs. N and Cu co-doping have significant effects on the electron receiving/electron giving capacity and electrical conductivity [30]. Inspired by these studies, we hope to effectively adjust the optical properties of CDs through metal doping, and further expand the application of CDs materials in fluorescence sensing.

The citric acid (CA) and thiourea (TU) were obtained from Kuer Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Tetracycline (TC, 99%) was got from Shanghai Yien Chemical Technology Co., LTD. Norfloxacin (NOR, 99%), ofloxacin (OFX, 98%), sulfamethoxazole (SMZ, 98%), enrofloxacin (ENR, 98%) andciprofloxacin (CIP, 98%) were obtained from Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaCl, KCl, ZnCl2, HgCl2, AlCl3, CrCl3·6H2O, FeCl3, MgCl2·6H2O, CaSO4, Na2CO3, BaCl2·2H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, Pb(NO3)2, CuSO4·5H2O, K3PO4, K2Cr2O7 as metal ion sources were purchased from Shanghai Sinopharmic Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals in the experiment were not further purified, and ultra-pure water was used for subsequent experiments.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the as-prepared Cu-NCDs and CDs were obtained using TEM (FEI Tecnai G2 F20, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated by ESCALAB 250Xi spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Infrared analyses were completed by the Fourier-transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (PerkinElmer, USA). Visual detection under 365 nm was performed on a UV lamp (Qi Wei, China). Experimental studies on 3D FL of Cu-NCDs were recorded by Hitachi F-7000 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Experimental studies on the determination of Fe3+ and TC by FL were recorded by a Steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, FLS 1000-STM, UK). Raman spectra were recorded using a customized Raman microscope equipped with a Pixis-100BR CCD (Princeton Instruments, USA).

Firstly, citric acid (CA), thiourea (TU) and CuSO4·5H2O (mole ratio 5:15:1) were dissolved in 5 mL water and mixed evenly, then placed in an oil bath to prepare Cu-NCDs by direct pyrolysis. The CA/TU/CuSO4·5H2O aqueous solution was ultrasonically treated for 10 min, then transferred to a circular bottomed bottle and heated at 180 ℃ for 1 h. After the reaction was completed, the resulting dark brown coagulated substance was cooled to solid state, then the crude products obtained were dissolved by adding ultrapure water and transferred to the centrifugal tube for centrifugation at 10,000 RPM for 5 min. The resulting supernatant filtrate was put into a dialysis bag (Mw cut-off: 1Kda) 24 h to remove small molecules in the solution. Preparation ofCDs: The molar ratio (1:3) of CA and TU were dissolved in 5 mL ultrapure water. The remaining steps are the same as those used to prepare Cu-NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL detection of Fe3+ and TC
ce:section.ce:para: For this, 250 μL Cu-NCDs were diluted into 1500 μL using Britton-Robison (PH = 4, pH = 10) solution. The blank sample was determined at λ ex = 362 nm, and the FL intensity of the Cu-NCDs λ em = 450 nm was measured. Add different concentrations of Fe3+ (250 μL) solutioninto the above solution (pH = 4), mixed well, and the FL intensity of the samples was measured at room temperature. Then, the FL spectra were recorded after 1 min. Similarly, a series of concentrations of TC were added to the FL probe, and then the probe was diluted with Britton-Robison solution (pH = 10) to 1500 μL. At room temperature, the FL intensity of the samples was measured after 30 min. The effects of other antibiotics (30 μM) and other interfering ions (Al3+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, K+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Na+, Cr6+, Cl-, SO4 2-, CO3 2–, PO4 3-, 60 μM) on the FL intensity of Cu-NCDs were compared under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ and TC in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Lake water and the tap water samples as actual samples were obtained from Southwest Jiaotong University (Xi Pu Campus). The collected water samples were pretreated (filtered by 0.22 μm membrane) to remove small molecular suspended solids.. Firstly, for the detection of Fe3+, several concentrations of Fe3+ (60, 200, 500 μM) were added to the filtration of lake water and tap water and kept the volume to 1500 μL. Moreover, H2O2 (500 μM) was added to the mixture above when detecting indirectly Fe2+ in the system. Similarly, different concentrations of TC (5, 15, 50 μM) were added to the filtrate when TC was examined.

In order to further study the morphology and surface structure of the Cu-NCDs and CDs, the prepared materials were tested by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). According to Fig. 2 a, The Cu-NCDs were spherical in shape with a size range of 1–10 nm and an average partical size of around 4.8 nm. The TEM image (Fig. 2b) was CDs image with an average particle diameter of 3.9 nm. It showed that Cu doping increased the particle size of CDs.

The surface states of Cu-NCDs were further studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Four characteristic peaks (285 eV (C1s), 400 eV (N1s), 532 eV (O1s), and 933 eV (Cu2p)) were observed in the Cu-NCDs X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 3 a). For the C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3b), peaks at 288.0 eV and 284.8 eV represent two groups of O–C = O and C–C, individually. The peak of N1s was found at 399.7 eV, (Fig. 3c). The O1s had two peaks of 538.4 eV and 531.6 eV (Fig. 3e), which combined with C1s and confirmed the existence of oxidized carbon in Cu-NCDs. The Cu2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 3f) revealed two comparatively weaker peaks at 952.2 eV and 933.1 eV confirming the Cu-doping effectively. The Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 existed around 933.1 eV and 952.2 eV severally, indicating that Cu existed in the Cu-NCDs as Cu+ state [31]. Moreover, there were no peaks at 962 eV and 943 eV, and the characteristics of Cu2+ further indicated that the Cu doped in the Cu-NCDs was in a Cu+ state, which was ascributed to the higher reactivity of Cu+ [19,32]. FT-IR spectrum of Cu-NCDs showed the broad peaks of 3000–3500 cm−1 were O–H and N–H. This was consistent with the observation of XPS that carbon oxide may be linked in the form of carbon with an –OH group. As the FT-IR spectrogram (Fig. 3d) showed that the characteristic peaks at 2764 cm−1, 2060 cm−1, 1688 cm-1confirmed the presence of C–H, C = O, and C = N, respectively. Furthermore, a C-N bond at 1398 cm-1C-O bond at 1188 cm−1 was also observed. Combining the above results with the XPS and FT-IR results, the presence of the –OH group on Cu-NCDs surface provided excellent water solubility.

UV–vis absorption spectrum FL spectra investigated the optical properties of as-prepared Cu-NCDs. As exhibited in Fig. 4 a, there was a prominent absorption peak at 343 nm in the UV–vis spectrum, which may be the n-π* transition peak of the C = O bond [33]. The Cu-NCDs aqueous solution is light brown and transparent under sunlight, while it exhibited blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation (inset of Fig. S1). Interestingly, compared with CDs and other metals-doped CDs, the Cu-NCDs showed the strongest fluorescence (Fig. S1). The relative quantum yields of five carbon dots were determined by using quinine sulfate solution at the optimum excitation wavelength (λ ex = 362 nm) as a reference sample. The results were as follows: Cu-NCDs (53.6%), CDs (37.7%), Ce-NCDs (30.7%), Gd-NCDs (44.9%) and Zr-NCDs (46.5%), respectively. It can be seen from the determination results that the prepared Cu-NCDs had a high QY and excellent optical performance. This suggested that the Cu doping may change the structure and surface state of the carbon core, leading to the enhancement of fluorescence emission intensity. To explore Cu-NCDs' fluorescence origin, we tested that there was a maximum emission wavelength of 450 nm at the excitation wavelength of 362 nm in Fig. 4a. Compared with pure CDs, only the fluorescence intensity of Cu-NCDs increased without a shift of peak positions, seen from Fig. S2. In addition, as shown in Fig. 4b, the Cu-NCDs had a strong fluorescence emission at 450 nm, and fluorescence attenuation occurred obviously after adding Fe3+ (Fig. 4c) and TC (Fig. 4d), respectively.

As mentioned above, the Cu-NCDs were prepared by direct pyrolysis. Several reaction conditions were optimized: CA/TU/ CuSO4·5H2O mole ratios, reaction temperature, and time. Firstly, the ratio of CuSO4‘5H2O to CA/TU from 0.05 to 0.5, when other parameters were kept unchanged. According to the Fig. S3a, b, the best ratio of 0.2 was obtained. The fluorescence intensity of Cu-NCDs decreased with a lower or higher source ratio. The higher proportions of fluorescence reduction may be attributed to excess Cu’s blockage of the passivated surface defects in the inclusion [32]. The reaction temperature of as-synthesized Cu-NCDs was also investigated. The results showed that carbon dots prepared at 180 ℃ (Fig. S3c, d) had the best effect on detecting the target substance.

Similarly, the reaction time of the preparation was also studied to obtain the optimal Cu-NCDs. As can be observed in Fig. S3e, f, the Cu-NCDs synthesized by 1 h reaction showed the highest fluorescence, indicating that 1 h was the best experimental condition. Under these conditions, efficient doping of Cu appeared to prepare Cu-NCDs with stable blue fluorescence and high fluorescence intensity.

To achieve the optimal detection of Fe3+ and TC, the response time, pH value, and reaction temperature were investigated, respectively. To obtain an optimum pH condition, the pH from 2 to 9 and from 2 to 11 were achieved by using Britton-Robison solution to detect Fe3+ and TC, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 a, d, pH 4 for Fe3+ and pH 10 for TC were selected. In Fig. 5b, f, the response time had little effect on the detection of Fe3+. However, the reaction time tended to be stable after 30 min, and the quenching effect was the best when TC was detected. Hence, 30 min was chosen for convenient. In Fig. 5c, h, although the quenching effect of Fe3+ and TC was the highest at 50 ℃ and 30 ℃ respectively, the peaks of both the Cu-NCDs solution and the mixture added to the target decreased with the increase of temperature from 20 to 70 ℃, which could be seen in Fig. S4. Therefore, to avoid the influence of temperature on the system, the detection in this study was carried out at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ and TC sensing with Cu-NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the addition of Fe3+ and TC both could carry significantly FL quenching of Cu-NCDs. As seen in Fig. 6 a, b, the fluorescence signal at F 450nm gradually weakened with the increase of Fe3+ concentration over the range of 10–1500 μM, the linear fit had two segment: F 0/F 1 = 0.00235 [Fe3+] + 1.0209 (10–400 μM, R2 = 0.997, n = 3) and F 0/F 1 = 0.0012 [Fe3+] + 1.4627 (400–1500 μM, R2 = 0.991, n = 3), with the detection limit (LOD) of 1.189 μM. Moreover, the performance of Cu-NCDs for sensing TC was studied by collecting fluorescence of Cu-NCDs with different concentrations of TC. Fig. 6c displayed the fluorescence intensity of F 450nm showed a linear decrease upon increasing of TC from 1 to 80 μM, the linear fit was F 0/F 1 = 0.0237 [TC] + 0.9493 (R2 = 0.994, n = 3), with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.416 μM.

Interestingly, in the experimental process, it was found that H2O2 had almost no effect on carbon points when added to the system, but it could transform Fe2+ into Fe3+ to quench the Cu-NCDs. Therefore, the indirect determination of Fe2+ was realized by adding H2O2 into the system to detect Fe3+ (Fig. S5). Its linear fluorescence spectrum was as shown in Fig. S6.

For practical application, the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the proposed multi-multifunctional fluorescent sensor were explored. As shown in Fig. S7, when the analytes were replaced by some common ions (e.g., Co2+, Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Zn2+, Cr6+, CO3 2–, Cl-, PO4 3- and SO4 2-) or different antibiotics (e.g., OTC), the fluorescence intensity of the sensor showed negligible changes, revealing that Cu-NCDs had good selectivity towards Fe3+ and TC detection. To study the anti-interference ability of the sensor, different ions (Al3+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Zn2+, Cr6+, CO3 2–, Cl-, PO4 3-, SO4 2-, the concentration of all added ions were 60 μM) were introduced into the mixture with Fe3+ and TC, respectively. The results (Fig. 6e, f) demonstrated that the multi-multifunctional fluorescent sensor had a strong anti-interference ability in detecting the target analytes. The concentrations (60 μM) of various anions added in this experiment are much higher than those contained in environmental water [34], which proved that the prepared Cu-NCDs were ideal candidates for selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ or TC in environmental water.

To explore Cu-NCDs’ fluorescence quenching mechanism, we further measured UV–Vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, and fluorescence lifetimes. As seen in Fig. 7 a, the excitation peak of the Cu-NCDs partially overlapped the absorption spectrum of Fe3+, which indicated that the internal filtration effect (IFE) occurred between Cu-NCDs and Fe3+. In addition, the excitation spectra of Cu-NCDs and absorption spectra of TC overlapped completely (Fig. 7b), indicating that there was an effective IFE between TC and Cu-NCDs. TC could absorb the excitation light of Cu-NCDs with a high molar absorption coefficient to cause fluorescence quenching of Cu-NCDs. The results showed that IFE was a nondeductible mechanism for Cu-NCDs quenching by Fe3+ and TC [35]. To further explore the main causes of fluorescence quenching, the lifetimes of Cu-NCDs before and after quenching were measured. The fluorescence attenuation curve was shown in Fig. 7c,d. Cu-NCDs' fluorescence lifetimes and the mixed solution after adding Fe3+ were 8.5 ns and 7.04 ns, respectively. The fluorescence quenching between Cu-NCDs and Fe3+ was dynamic. Similarly, the fluorescence lifetime of Cu-NCDs and the mixed solution with TC were 8.82 ns and 8.54 ns, respectively, indicating almost no apparent electron transfer between Cu-NCDs and TC. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of Cu-NCDs could be attributed to mainly static quenching based on IFE.

The quenching efficiency was analyzed with the Stern-Volmer equation(
F 0
/
F 1
= 1 _ K s v
c
= 1 + K q ·
τ 0
c
), where K sv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, [c] is the concentration of Fe3+ or TC, Kq is the dynamic quenching rate constant, τ0 is the average lifetime of a fluorescent substance (usually of the order of 10-8 s), F0 and F1 are the fluorescence intensities at 450 nm in the absence and presence of target analytes, respectively. The dynamic quenching constant increases with the increase of temperature, while the static quenching constant decreases with the increase of temperature, and the fluorescence lifetime decreases with the addition of quenching agent [36,37]. There was a good linear relationship between F0/F1 and [Q], and the slope of the line in the Fig. 8 was K sv. The Stern-Volmer quenching constant K sv and the bimolecular quenching constant Kq were calculated according to the Stern-Volmer equation. As shown in Table 1 , the quenching constant increased obviously with the increase of temperature, and it was considered that the quenching of Cu-NCDs by Fe3+ was dynamic quenching. As can be seen from Fig. 8b, Stern-Volmer curves at three temperatures showed a good linear relationship, and K sv decreased with the increase of temperature, which was exactly consistent with the static quenching mechanism.

This method was applied to water samples recognize multiple objects (shown in Table S1-3). The detection methods were described in the experiment section. The recoveries of Fe3+ were 98.2–100.9%, with RSDs of 0.7–1.3% in lake water and 96.1–100.1%, with RSDs of 0.7–1.5% in tap water. Moreover, when Fe2+ and Fe3+ were simultaneously added to the actual water sample, and then H2O2 was introduced into the mixture, the content of Fe2+ could be calculated by simply analyzing the data obtained by adding H2O2 or not in the system. Similarly, we also conducted a feasibility study on TC. The recoveries from 96.7% to 98.0% with RSD from 1.6% to 5.6% in lake water and from 96.0% to 100.7% with RSD from 1.8% to 2.0 % in tap water were obtained.Therefore, the above research proved that Cu-NCDs sensor had great potential in the field of environmental analysis. Moreover, compared with other methods (Table 2 ), the Cu-NCDs in this study had a wide detection range, low detection limits and it could be used as a multifunctional fluorescent sensor. Compared with other preparation methods, this method had low cost, simple synthesis process and low energy consumption. For example, the synthesis of F-CDs required heating at 200℃ for 8 h [40], while the synthesis of Cu-NCDs in this experiment only took 1 h. In addition, the doping of N and Cu greatly improved the fluorescence quantum yield, reaching 53.6%, which provided an idea for the preparation of new carbon dots with high fluorescence quantum yield.

In summary, a multi-functional sensor method with a simple preparation and high detection efficiency for Cu-doped carbon dots was established. Due to the doping of Cu, the fluorescence of the resultant Cu-NCDs was greatly improved, and it exhibited a good selectivity to detect Fe3+ and TC. In the presence of Fe3+ and TC, the fluorescence of Cu-NCDs was quenched owing to the IFE, which provides a basis for the quantitative detection of Fe3+ and TC. In addition, because H2O2 could transform Fe2+ into Fe3+, the indirect determination of Fe2+ was realized by adding H2O2 into the system. The Cu-NCDs sensor has been applied to the two targets detection in the water samples, successfully. The recoveries of lake water and tap water by the investigated Fe3+ and TC were 96.1–100.9% and 96.0–100.7%, respectively. The results show that Cu-NCDs, as a multi-functional sensor, have certain accuracy and satisfactory reproducibility, providing a unique way for the determination of Fe3+ and TC in environmental water analysis.

Jing Liu: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Qiumeng Chen: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Zhixiao Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Zhaoli Wang: Supervision. Zhengjun Gong: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22176154 & 21777131) and Science and Technology Department Foundation of Sichuan Province (No. 22ZDYF1945). Thanks to the Analytical and Testing Center of Southwest Jiaotong University for the support of experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107628.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene utilizing fluorescent sensor from carbon dots and reusable magnetic core-shell nanomaterial
